source_paper_title: Fast Combinatorial Algorithms for Simultaneously Approximating All ℓp-Norms in Correlation Clustering
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Davies_2025, title={Fast Combinatorial Algorithms for Simultaneously Approximating All ℓp-Norms in Correlation Clustering}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0567}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0567}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Davies, Sami and Moseley, Benjamin and Newman, Heather}, year={2025}, month=sept }
context:
Page 17, Section 5 (Conclusion).

One question is whether there is a simpler existential (not necessarily algorithmic) proof that there exists an \(O(1)\)-approximation for the all-norm objective for correlation clustering.
title: Prove existence of a simpler all-norms approximation clustering for correlation clustering
background_latex:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be an unweighted complete graph on \(n=|V|\) vertices whose edges are labeled \(+\) (similar) or \(-\) (dissimilar). Write \(E^+\) and \(E^-\) for the sets of positive and negative edges, respectively.

A clustering \(\mathcal C\) is a partition of \(V\) into clusters. For a vertex \(u\in V\), let \(\mathcal C(u)\) denote the cluster containing \(u\), and let \(V\setminus \mathcal C(u)\) be the vertices in other clusters.

An edge \(\{u,v\}\in E^+\) is a disagreement under \(\mathcal C\) if \(u\) and \(v\) lie in different clusters; an edge \(\{u,v\}\in E^-\) is a disagreement if \(u\) and \(v\) lie in the same cluster.

Define the disagreement count (disagreement degree) of \(u\) under \(\mathcal C\) by
\[
 y_{\mathcal C}(u) := |\{v\in V: \{u,v\}\text{ is a disagreement under }\mathcal C\}|,
\]
and the disagreement vector \(y_{\mathcal C}\in \mathbb Z_{\ge 0}^n\) by \((y_{\mathcal C}(u))_{u\in V}\).

For \(p\in [1,\infty)\), define \(\|y_{\mathcal C}\|_p := \big(\sum_{u\in V} y_{\mathcal C}(u)^p\big)^{1/p}\), and \(\|y_{\mathcal C}\|_{\infty} := \max_{u\in V} y_{\mathcal C}(u)\).

For each \(p\in [1,\infty]\), let
\[
\mathrm{OPT}_p := \min_{\mathcal C\text{ a partition of }V}\ \|y_{\mathcal C}\|_p
\]
denote the optimal \(\ell_p\)-norm disagreement objective value.

A clustering \(\mathcal C\) is said to be a constant-factor approximation simultaneously for all \(\ell_p\)-objectives (the all-norms objective) if there exists a universal constant \(\alpha>0\) such that for every \(p\in [1,\infty]\), \(\|y_{\mathcal C}\|_p \le \alpha\,\mathrm{OPT}_p\).
problem_definition_latex:
Give a proof, not necessarily algorithmic, that for every unweighted complete signed graph \(G=(V,E)\) as above, there exists a clustering \(\mathcal C\) and a universal constant \(\alpha>0\) (independent of \(n\), the instance \(G\), and \(p\)) such that
\[
\|y_{\mathcal C}\|_p \le \alpha\,\mathrm{OPT}_p \qquad \text{for all } p\in [1,\infty].
\]
The goal is to obtain a simpler existential argument for the existence of such a clustering than the paper's algorithmic construction and analysis.
latex:

and the disagreement vector \(y_{\mathcal C}\in \mathbb Z_{\ge 0}^n\) by \((y_{\mathcal C}(u))_{u\in V}\).

Give a proof, not necessarily algorithmic, that for every unweighted complete signed graph \(G=(V,E)\) as above, there exists a clustering \(\mathcal C\) and a universal constant \(\alpha>0\) (independent of \(n\), the instance \(G\), and \(p\)) such that

\|y_{\mathcal C}\|_p \le \alpha\,\mathrm{OPT}_p \qquad \text{for all } p\in [1,\infty].

open_problem_quote: One question is whether there is a simpler existential (not necessarily algorithmic) proof that there exists an \(O(1)\)-approximation for the all-norm objective for correlation clustering.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for an existential proof, so negative instances are not relevant unless one is trying to refute the statement; but the paper already proves existence constructively. There is no clear finite search space where counterexamples would meaningfully advance the stated goal of simplifying the proof.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: A coding-agent loop could search for alternative, simpler constructions (e.g., randomized or greedy clusterings) and empirically test the achieved approximation ratios over many generated instances and multiple \(p\)-values as a heuristic fitness signal. However, translating empirical patterns into a genuinely simpler general proof may still be nontrivial, so suitability is not high.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The all-norms question for correlation clustering on unweighted complete signed graphs is answered constructively by "Simultaneously Approximating All \(\ell_p\)-norms in Correlation Clustering", which produces a single clustering \(\mathcal C\) with \(\|y_{\mathcal C}\|_p\le \alpha\,\mathrm{OPT}_p\) for every \(p\in[1,\infty]\). The proof builds a single semimetric (an adjusted version of the earlier correlation metric) whose fractional disagreement cost is within a universal constant of \(\mathrm{OPT}_p\) for every \(p\), and then applies the KMZ ball-growing rounding scheme, which is independent of \(p\). This establishes existence of an all-norms constant-factor clustering, but via a fairly intricate algorithmic construction and charging analysis.

Earlier work by Davies–Moseley–Newman on the \(\ell_\infty\) (min–max) objective introduced the correlation metric, a Jaccard-type distance based on overlap of positive neighborhoods, and showed it has bounded fractional cost for \(p=\infty\); this metric is the structural seed for the all-norms result. The KMZ framework ("Correlation clustering with local objectives") supplies the key rounding lemma: given any (approximate) semimetric with controlled fractional costs, the same rounding procedure yields a clustering with controlled per-vertex disagreements, hence controlled \(\ell_p\) norms. Conceptually, this reduces the all-norms existence question to the existence of a single semimetric that simultaneously upper-bounds the relevant fractional objectives.

As posed in the source paper’s conclusion, the remaining open aspect is not whether such a clustering exists (it does), but whether there is a substantially simpler, non-algorithmic existential proof—e.g., one that avoids constructing an adjusted metric and the detailed per-vertex charging needed to show \(p\)-uniform bounds. Promising directions suggested by the current proofs include: (i) identifying an intrinsic structural certificate (perhaps via bad-triangle duality or a majorization argument on disagreement vectors) that implies simultaneous \(\ell_p\) control; or (ii) proving that an appropriately chosen distribution over simple clusterings (e.g., Pivot-like) contains a single clustering achieving all-norms guarantees, via probabilistic method and concentration/union bounds tailored to all \(p\).

sources.title: Simultaneously Approximating All ℓp-norms in Correlation Clustering
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives a single clustering that is an \(O(1)\)-approximation simultaneously for all \(\ell_p\)-norm objectives \(p\in[1,\infty]\) on unweighted complete signed graphs, via an adjusted correlation metric fed into KMZ rounding.
sources.connection: Directly establishes existence (constructively) of a universal constant-factor all-norms approximation clustering; the open problem asks for a simpler existential proof than this algorithmic construction.

sources.title: Correlation clustering with local objectives
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces an LP/convex relaxation for \(\ell_p\)-norm disagreement objectives and a ball-growing rounding algorithm (KMZ rounding) giving a constant-factor approximation (not universal across \(p\) because the relaxation depends on \(p\)).
sources.connection: Provides the rounding framework used by the all-norms algorithm; any existential proof may still leverage the idea that a single semimetric can be rounded into a clustering with controlled per-vertex disagreements.

sources.title: Fast Combinatorial Algorithms for Min Max Correlation Clustering
sources.theorem_or_result: Defines the correlation metric \(d\) (Jaccard-style distance on positive neighborhoods) and shows it is a semimetric with \(O(1)\) fractional cost for the \(\ell_\infty\) (min-max) objective; combined with KMZ rounding yields a constant-factor approximation for \(\ell_\infty\) without solving an LP.
sources.connection: Supplies the key structural object (correlation metric) that later gets adjusted to handle all \(p\); suggests a route to simpler existence proofs via semimetric constructions rather than LPs.

sources.title: Local Guarantees in Graph Cuts and Clustering
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies local (per-vertex) guarantees for cut/clustering objectives including correlation clustering; develops relaxations/rounding giving constant-factor approximations for local disagreement measures.
sources.connection: Early work motivating per-vertex disagreement vectors and local/fairness objectives that are endpoints of the all-norms spectrum (especially \(p=\infty\)).

sources.title: Aggregating inconsistent information: ranking and clustering
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the Pivot algorithm and a 3-approximation in expectation for \(\ell_1\) correlation clustering on complete graphs.
sources.connection: Represents the \(p=1\) endpoint; highlights that optimizing one norm (\(\ell_1\)) alone can be arbitrarily bad for other norms, motivating the all-norms question.

sources.title: All-norm approximation algorithms
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the all-norms objective in approximation algorithms and gives constant-factor all-norms approximations for load balancing variants.
sources.connection: Provides conceptual and technical precedent for simultaneous \(\ell_p\) approximation; may inspire non-algorithmic existence arguments via majorization or norm-monotonicity techniques.
